abundance: measure of how many fish are in a population or a fishing ground
acoustic survey: systematic gathering of information on fish availability and abundance using underwater sound
aggregation: any grouping of fish for whatever reason they are concentrating; see shoaling
agricultural runoff: surplus water from agricultural land, often enriched with nutrients and sediment
algal bloom: rapid excessive growth of algae, generally caused by high nutrient levels
anadromous: fish that live adult lives in the ocean but migrate up fresh water rivers to spawn
aquaculture: farming of freshwater and saltwater organisms including molluscs, crustaceans and aquatic plants
availability: proportion of a fish population living where it can be fished; or catch per unit effort
bait fish: small fish caught for use as bait to attract large predatory fish
bathypelagic: open ocean or pelagic zone from 1000 to 4000 meters below the ocean surface
beam trawling: simplest method of bottom trawling; net mouth held open by a solid metal beam
benthic zone: ecological region at the lowest level of a body of water including the sediment surface
benthos: aquatic organisms which live on or in the seabed
biodiversity: variation of life forms within an area; number and variety of organisms in a fishery
biomass: total weight of a fish species in a given area
bony fish: fish with bony skeleton belonging to class osteichthyes
bottom trawling: fishing method that involves towing trawl nets along the sea floor
brackish water: water with more salinity than fresh water but less than seawater
brood: collective offspring of a species produced in a particular time span; see cohort
bycatch: harvest of marine life during fishing when other fish were the target
carapace: calcified protective cover on the upper frontal surface of crustaceans
carrying capacity: supportable population of a species given food, habitat and other resources
catadromous: fish that live in fresh water but migrate down rivers to spawn in the sea
cephalopods: animals such as squid and octopus where tentacles converge at the head
cetacean: marine mammals including whales, dolphins and porpoises
cetacean bycatch: incidental capture of non-target cetacean species by fisheries
climate change: variation in global or regional climates over time from natural or human causes
cohort: individuals of a stock born in the same spawning season; a year-class
commercial fishery: umbrella term covering fisheries resources, catching, processing and marketing
conspecific: organisms or populations that belong to the same species
crustaceans: animals with jointed legs and hard shell made of chitin; crabs, lobsters, shrimp, krill
danish seine: commercial fishing using a small trawl net with long wire warps in a circle
dead zone: area in ocean or lake where oxygen levels are extremely low, often from eutrophication
demersal zone: zone at or near the bottom of a sea or lake
demersal fish: fish that live in or near the bottom of a sea or lake; e.g. cod, flounder
depletion: reducing the abundance of a fish stock through fishing
diatoms: minute planktonic unicellular or colonial algae
downwelling: downward sinking of surface water caused by Ekman transport or converging currents
dredging: dredge towed along the bottom to catch scallops, oysters or sea cucumbers
driftnet: gillnet suspended by floats fishing the top few metres; can be many km long
dropline: fishing line with hooks held vertically with weights on continental shelf and slope
echinoderms: marine animals including seastars, sea urchins and sea cucumbers
ecologically sustainable development: using fishery resources so ecological processes are not degraded
ectothermic: animals controlling body temperature through external means
ekman transport: flow at right angles to wind direction (right in N hemisphere, left in S)
endangered species: population at risk of becoming extinct
estuary: semi-enclosed coastal body of water with free connection to the sea and diluted seawater
eutrophication: excessive richness of nutrients in water, often causing algal bloom and oxygen depletion
fecundity: reproductive capacity; number of eggs produced per female per spawning season
fish farming: commercial raising of fish in tanks or enclosures for food; see aquaculture
fishery: activity leading to harvesting of fish; includes the stock, fishermen, and business
fishing effort: measure of how much fishing is done; usually gear amount x time
fishing mortality: rate at which fish are removed from a population by fishing; denoted F
food chain: sequence of organisms each using the next lower member as a food source
food web: network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem
forage fish: small fish preyed on by larger predators; e.g. anchovies, sardines
ghost net: lost or abandoned fishing net continuing to catch and kill marine life
gillnet: net that catches fish by their gills; can be set, drift, or encircling
growth overfishing: fishing so many small fish that yield per recruit is reduced
habitat: place where an organism lives; its living environment
harmful algal bloom: bloom producing toxins harmful to marine life and humans
harvest: total number or weight of fish caught and kept from a fishery
high seas: ocean areas beyond national jurisdiction; outside any EEZ
hypoxia: low oxygen levels in water; often leads to dead zones
incidental catch: catch of non-targeted species; see bycatch
individual transferable quota: management tool allocating shares of TAC to individuals or companies
landing: amount of fish brought to shore and sold
longline: fishing line with many baited hooks, can be many km long
mariculture: cultivation of marine organisms in the open ocean or enclosures; marine aquaculture
maximum economic yield: catch level providing maximum economic profit in a fishery
maximum sustainable yield: largest average catch continuously taken from a stock under existing conditions
migration: movement of fish from one area to another, often for feeding or breeding
mortality: rate of death in a fish population from all causes
natural mortality: death rate from natural causes: predation, disease, old age; denoted M
ocean current: continuous directed movement of ocean water; surface or deep
otolith: calcium carbonate structures in fish inner ear; used for age determination
overfishing: fishing exceeding the capacity of the stock to replenish; leads to depletion
pelagic zone: open water zone away from the bottom of the sea or lake
pelagic fish: fish spending most of their life swimming in the water column; e.g. tuna, mackerel
phytoplankton: tiny free-floating photosynthetic organisms in aquatic systems
plankton: drifting organisms inhabiting pelagic zones of oceans or fresh water
population dynamics: study of fish populations: fishing mortality, growth, recruitment, natural mortality
precautionary principle: burden of proof falls on those advocating action if harm may be severe
predator-prey: predator feeds on prey; fundamental ecological relationship
primary productivity: measurement of plant production starting the food chain
purse seine: technique surrounding surface-schooling pelagic fish with a net that can be closed
quota: amount of catch legally landed in a time period; can be fishery-wide or individual
recruitment: number of new young fish entering a population in a given year
red tide: discoloration of surface waters from large concentrations of microorganisms
salinity: concentration of dissolved salts in water; measured in parts per thousand
selectivity: ability of fishing gear to catch certain size or kind of fish vs others
shoaling: behaviour of fish aggregating together for defence, foraging, or mating
spawning: production or depositing of large quantities of eggs in water
species: group of organisms capable of interbreeding and producing fertile offspring
stock: group of fish of the same species occupying a defined area; basis of fishery management
straddling stocks: fish stocks migrating between EEZ of one or more states and the high seas
sustainable fishing: fishing not causing undesirable changes in productivity, diversity, or ecosystem
sustainable yield: catch removable indefinitely without depleting the stock
total allowable catch: total catch allowed to be taken from a fishery in a given period
transboundary stocks: fish stocks ranging across EEZs of two or more countries
trawling: fishing with a large bag-like net drawn behind a boat; bottom or pelagic
trophic level: position a species occupies in a food chain
trolling: fishing with lines baited with lures drawn slowly behind a boat
turtle excluder device: device allowing captured sea turtles to escape from trawl nets
turbidity: measure of particle level (sediments, plankton) in water; affects clarity
upwelling: process where cold nutrient-rich water rises from deeper to shallower depth
vertebrates: animals with backbone including fish, amphibians, reptiles and mammals
vessel monitoring system: technology tracking location of commercial fishing vessels
virtual population analysis: analysis using catch-at-age and natural mortality to estimate fishing mortality
vulnerable species: species likely to become endangered unless threats improve
wetlands: areas of land where soil is saturated with moisture; swamps, mangroves
wild fisheries: also capture fisheries; target wild fish, contrasted with farmed fisheries
